items.title: Streaming API Reference
items.text: Complete REST API reference for the deprecated local generator's legacy streaming endpoints. The current Fabric live path is stream-events.ipynb writing directly to Eventhouse; see Phase 6.

items.title: Base URL
items.text: http://localhost:8000/api

items.title: Authentication
items.text: Currently no authentication required for local development. For production deployments, implement authentication middleware.

items.title: Start Streaming
items.text:
Start legacy real-time event streaming to the configured target.

POST /stream/start

Request Body:

{
  "duration_minutes": null,
  "event_types": ["receipt_created", "inventory_updated"],
  "burst_override": 100,
  "emit_interval_override": 500
}

Parameters: - duration_minutes (integer, optional): Auto-stop after N minutes. Null = run indefinitely - event_types (array, optional): Filter specific event types (must be valid EventType values). Null = all types - burst_override (integer, optional): Override the configured burst size for this session - emit_interval_override (integer, optional): Override the configured emit interval (ms) for this session

Streaming uses the outbox pattern: the background task drains pending streaming_outbox rows in timestamp order, and generates the next day of fact data only when the outbox is empty. See Streaming Workflow.

Response (200 OK):

{
  "success": true,
  "message": "Event streaming started",
  "operation_id": "streaming_a1b2c3d4",
  "started_at": "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z"
}

Error Responses:

  • 409 Conflict: Streaming already active
  • 400 Bad Request: Invalid event types or missing fact data
  • 400 Bad Request: Azure connection string not configured

Example:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/stream/start \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "emit_interval_override": 1000,
    "burst_override": 50,
    "duration_minutes": 10
  }'

items.title: Stop Streaming
items.text:
Stop the currently active streaming session.

POST /stream/stop

Response (200 OK):

{
  "success": true,
  "message": "Event streaming stopped",
  "operation_id": "streaming_a1b2c3d4"
}

Error Responses:

  • 400 Bad Request: No active streaming session
  • 404 Not Found: Streaming session not found

Example:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/stream/stop

items.title: Get Streaming Status
items.text:
Get current streaming status and basic statistics.

GET /stream/status

Response (200 OK):

{
  "is_streaming": true,
  "status": "running",
  "uptime_seconds": 450.5,
  "events_sent": 15234,
  "events_per_second": 33.8,
  "last_event_time": "2024-01-15T10:37:30Z"
}

Status Values: - running: Actively streaming events - stopped: No active streaming session - error: Streaming encountered errors

Example:

curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/status

items.title: Pause / Resume Streaming
items.text:
Pause an active streaming session without losing state, then resume it later.

POST /stream/pause
POST /stream/resume
GET /stream/pause-status

Pause status response (200 OK):

{
  "is_paused": false,
  "pause_count": 0,
  "total_pause_duration_seconds": 0.0,
  "currently_paused_duration": 0.0
}

Error Responses: - 400 Bad Request: No active streamer (start streaming first), or not in a pausable/resumable state

Get comprehensive streaming statistics and metrics.

GET /stream/statistics

Response (200 OK):

Example:

items.title: Get Recent Events
items.text:
Retrieve the most recent streaming events (last 100 events buffered).

GET /stream/events/recent?limit=50

Query Parameters: - limit (integer): Number of events to return (1-100). Default: 100

Response (200 OK):

{
  "events": [
    {
      "timestamp": "2024-01-15T10:40:15Z",
      "event_type": "receipt_created",
      "trace_id": "TR_20240115_abc123",
      "payload": {

items.title: Update Streaming Configuration
items.text:
Update streaming configuration settings.

PUT /stream/config

Request Body:

{
  "emit_interval_ms": 1000,
  "burst": 50,
  "max_batch_size": 128
}

Parameters (all optional): - emit_interval_ms (integer): Time between bursts - burst (integer): Events per burst - azure_connection_string (string): Legacy Event Hub connection string - max_batch_size (integer): Max events per batch - batch_timeout_ms (integer): Batch timeout

Other realtime settings (retry attempts, circuit breaker, etc.) are updated via PUT /api/config with a full configuration object.

Response (200 OK):

{
  "success": true,
  "message": "Streaming configuration updated successfully"
}

Error Responses:

  • 409 Conflict: Cannot update while streaming is active
  • 400 Bad Request: Invalid configuration values

Example:

curl -X PUT http://localhost:8000/api/stream/config \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"emit_interval_ms": 2000, "burst": 25}'

items.title: Event Envelope Format
items.text: All events use a standard envelope format for consistency and traceability.

items.title: Envelope Schema
items.text:
{
  "event_type": "receipt_created",
  "payload": {
    "store_id": 42,
    "customer_id": 15678,
    "receipt_id": "RCT_20240115_xyz789",
    "subtotal": 78.50,
    "tax": 6.92,
    "total": 85.42,
    "tender_type": "CREDIT_CARD",
    "item_count": 8
  },
  "trace_id": "TR_20240115_abc123xyz",
  "ingest_timestamp": "2024-01-15T10:40:15.123Z",
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "source": "retail-datagen",
  "correlation_id": "SESSION_xyz789",
  "partition_key": "store_42"
}

Envelope Fields: - event_type (string): Event type identifier (see Event Types section) - payload (object): Event-specific data - trace_id (string): Unique trace identifier for tracking - ingest_timestamp (string): ISO-8601 timestamp when event was created - schema_version (string): Event schema version (default: "1.0") - source (string): Source system (default: "retail-datagen") - correlation_id (string, optional): Links related events - partition_key (string, optional): Partition key for the legacy streaming target

items.title: Rate Limiting
items.text:
Some endpoints have rate limiting to prevent abuse:

  • /stream/start: 5 requests per 60 seconds
  • /stream/test: 10 requests per 60 seconds
  • /stream/validate-connection: 20 requests per 60 seconds
  • /config/validate: 20 requests per 60 seconds
  • /disruption/create: 20 requests per 60 seconds

Rate limit exceeded response (429):

{
  "detail": "Rate limit exceeded. Try again later."
}

items.title: Error Responses
items.text:
Standard error response format:

{
  "detail": "Error message describing the issue"
}

Common HTTP Status Codes: - 200 OK: Request successful - 400 Bad Request: Invalid request parameters - 404 Not Found: Resource not found - 409 Conflict: Conflicting state (e.g., streaming already active) - 429 Too Many Requests: Rate limit exceeded - 500 Internal Server Error: Server-side error - 501 Not Implemented: Feature not yet implemented

items.title: WebSocket Support (Future)
items.text:
WebSocket support for real-time event streaming is planned for future releases:

ws://localhost:8000/ws/stream

This will enable browser-based real-time event monitoring without polling.

items.title: OpenAPI Documentation
items.text:
Interactive API documentation available at:

  • Swagger UI: http://localhost:8000/docs
  • ReDoc: http://localhost:8000/redoc
  • OpenAPI JSON: http://localhost:8000/openapi.json

items.title: Python
items.text:
import requests

# Start streaming
response = requests.post('http://localhost:8000/api/stream/start', json={
    'emit_interval_override': 1000,
    'burst_override': 50,
    'duration_minutes': 10
})
print(response.json())

# Get status
status = requests.get('http://localhost:8000/api/stream/status')
print(status.json())

# Stop streaming
stop = requests.post('http://localhost:8000/api/stream/stop')
print(stop.json())

items.title: JavaScript/Node.js
items.text:
const axios = require('axios');

const baseURL = 'http://localhost:8000/api';

// Start streaming
async function startStreaming() {
  const response = await axios.post(`${baseURL}/stream/start`, {
    emit_interval_override: 1000,
    burst_override: 50,
    duration_minutes: 10
  });
  console.log(response.data);
}

// Get status
async function getStatus() {
  const response = await axios.get(`${baseURL}/stream/status`);
  console.log(response.data);
}

// Stop streaming
async function stopStreaming() {
  const response = await axios.post(`${baseURL}/stream/stop`);
  console.log(response.data);
}

items.title: cURL
items.text:
# Start streaming
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/stream/start \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"emit_interval_override": 1000, "burst_override": 50, "duration_minutes": 10}'

# Get status
curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/status

# Stop streaming
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/stream/stop

    }
  }
}

items.title: 1. Event Throughput
items.text:
Metric: events_per_second Target: 20-50 events/sec (configurable based on needs) Alert: < 10 events/sec when streaming is active

How to check: 

curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/status | jq '.events_per_second'

items.title: 2. Success Rate
items.text:
Metric: (events_sent_successfully / events_generated) * 100 Target: > 99% Alert: < 95%

Calculate: 

curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/statistics | jq '(.events_sent_successfully / .events_generated) * 100'

items.title: 3. Failure Rate
items.text:
Metric: (events_failed / events_generated) * 100 Target: < 1% Alert: > 5%

Calculate: 

curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/statistics | jq '(.events_failed / .events_generated) * 100'

items.title: 4. Circuit Breaker Trips
items.text:
Metric: circuit_breaker_trips Target: 0 Alert: > 3 trips in 1 hour

How to check: 

curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/statistics | jq '.circuit_breaker_trips'

items.title: 5. Connection Failures
items.text:
Metric: connection_failures Target: 0 Alert: > 10 failures in 10 minutes

How to check: 

curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/statistics | jq '.connection_failures'

items.title: Legacy Target Metrics
items.text:
Monitor these in Azure Portal:

  • Incoming Messages: Should match events_sent_successfully
  • Throttled Requests: Should be 0 or minimal
  • Server Errors: Should be 0
  • User Errors: Check for 400/403/413 errors
  • Throughput Units Used: Monitor for capacity

Azure CLI: 

az monitor metrics list \
  --resource "/subscriptions/{subscription-id}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.EventHub/namespaces/{namespace}" \
  --metric "IncomingMessages" \
  --start-time 2024-01-15T10:00:00Z \
  --end-time 2024-01-15T11:00:00Z

items.title: Application Health
items.text:
Endpoint: GET /api/stream/health

Status Levels: - healthy: All systems operational - degraded: Some issues but still functional - unhealthy: Critical failures

Prometheus metrics:

The application exposes Prometheus metrics (streaming health, performance, errors, circuit breaker state) at GET /metrics:

# Prometheus scrape config
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'retail-datagen'
    metrics_path: '/metrics'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['localhost:8000']

items.title: Kubernetes Liveness Probe
items.text:
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /health
    port: 8000
  initialDelaySeconds: 30
  periodSeconds: 10
  failureThreshold: 3

items.title: Kubernetes Readiness Probe
items.text:
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /api/stream/health
    port: 8000
  initialDelaySeconds: 10
  periodSeconds: 5
  failureThreshold: 2

items.title: Issue 1: Connection Failures
items.text:
Symptoms: - High connection_failures count - Circuit breaker opens immediately - Events not reaching the legacy streaming target

Diagnosis: 

# Test connection
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/stream/test

# Check Azure config
curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/config | jq '.realtime.azure_connection_string'

Solutions:

  1. Verify connection string format: 

    # Should include: Endpoint, SharedAccessKeyName, SharedAccessKey
echo $AZURE_EVENTHUB_CONNECTION_STRING

  2. Check network connectivity: 

    # Test DNS resolution
nslookup your-namespace.servicebus.windows.net

# Test connectivity (port 5671 for AMQP)
telnet your-namespace.servicebus.windows.net 5671

  3. Verify Azure credentials:

  4. Check SharedAccessKey is correct
  5. Verify policy has Send permissions

  6. Check key hasn't expired

  7. Check firewall rules:

  8. Allow outbound connections to *.servicebus.windows.net
  9. Ports: 5671 (AMQP), 443 (HTTPS)

items.title: Issue 2: High Failure Rate
items.text:
Symptoms: - failure_rate > 5% - Many events in dead letter queue - Throttling errors in statistics

Diagnosis: 

# Check error breakdown
curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/statistics | jq '.error_counts'

# Check DLQ size
curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/dlq/summary | jq '.size'

Solutions:

  1. Legacy streaming target throttling:
  2. Check Azure metrics for throttled requests
  3. Reduce burst size: curl -X PUT http://localhost:8000/api/stream/config -d '{"burst": 50}'
  4. Increase emit_interval_ms: curl -X PUT http://localhost:8000/api/stream/config -d '{"emit_interval_ms": 1000}'

  5. Upgrade the target streaming tier if applicable

  6. Network congestion:

  7. Increase batch_timeout_ms to allow more time for sends
  8. Reduce max_batch_size to smaller batches

  9. Check network latency: ping your-namespace.servicebus.windows.net

  10. Application issues:

  11. Check application logs for exceptions
  12. Verify Python version (requires 3.11+)
  13. Check memory usage: ps aux | grep python

items.title: Issue 3: Circuit Breaker Opens Frequently
items.text:
Symptoms: - circuit_breaker_trips increasing - Streaming stops automatically - Status shows "error"

Diagnosis: 

# Check circuit breaker status
curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/statistics | jq '.circuit_breaker_trips'

# Check failure pattern
curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/statistics | jq '.error_counts'

Solutions:

  1. Increase failure threshold: 

    {
  "realtime": {
    "circuit_breaker_failure_threshold": 10,
    "circuit_breaker_enabled": true
  }
}

  2. Increase recovery timeout: 

    {
  "realtime": {
    "circuit_breaker_recovery_timeout": 120
  }
}

  3. Fix underlying connection issues (see Issue 1)

items.title: Issue 4: Low Throughput
items.text:
Symptoms: - events_per_second < 10 - Slow event generation - High uptime but low event count

Diagnosis: 

# Check current rate
curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/status | jq '.events_per_second'

# Check configuration
curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/config | jq '.realtime.emit_interval_ms, .realtime.burst'

Solutions:

  1. Increase burst size: 

    curl -X PUT http://localhost:8000/api/stream/config \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"burst": 200}'

  2. Decrease emit interval: 

    curl -X PUT http://localhost:8000/api/stream/config \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"emit_interval_ms": 250}'

  3. Check system resources: 

    # CPU usage
top -p $(pgrep -f retail_datagen)

# Memory usage
ps aux | grep retail_datagen

items.title: Issue 5: Events Not Appearing in Azure
items.text:
Symptoms: - events_sent_successfully shows high counts - Legacy streaming target shows no incoming messages - No errors reported

Diagnosis: 

# Verify legacy target name
curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/config | jq '.stream.hub'

# Check recent events
curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/events/recent?limit=5

Solutions:

  1. Verify legacy target name matches:
  2. Check stream.hub in config
  3. Verify the target exists in Azure Portal

  4. Check EntityPath in connection string

  5. Check target metrics:

  6. Navigate to the target's Azure Portal metrics
  7. Look at "Incoming Messages" (should match events_sent)

  8. Check "Throttled Requests" (should be 0)

  9. Verify connection string: 

    # Connection string should include EntityPath or hub name in config
echo $AZURE_EVENTHUB_CONNECTION_STRING | grep EntityPath

items.title: How It Works
items.text:
The circuit breaker prevents cascading failures by stopping send attempts when error rate is too high.

States: - Closed: Normal operation, all events sent - Open: Too many failures, reject all sends immediately - Half-Open: Testing if service recovered, allow limited sends

Configuration: 

{
  "realtime": {
    "circuit_breaker_enabled": true,
    "circuit_breaker_failure_threshold": 5,
    "circuit_breaker_recovery_timeout": 60
  }
}

Parameters: - circuit_breaker_failure_threshold: Failures before opening (default: 5) - circuit_breaker_recovery_timeout: Seconds to wait before attempting to close an open circuit (default: 60)

items.title: Monitoring Circuit Breaker
items.text:
# Check trips
curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/statistics | jq '.circuit_breaker_trips'

# Current state (check failure pattern)
curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/statistics | jq '.error_counts'

items.title: Manual Reset
items.text:
Stop and restart streaming to reset circuit breaker:

# Stop streaming
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/stream/stop

# Fix underlying issues

# Restart streaming
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/stream/start \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"emit_interval_ms": 1000, "burst": 50}'

items.title: Overview
items.text:
Failed events are stored in a dead letter queue (DLQ) for analysis and retry.

Retention: In-memory only, cleared on restart

items.title: View DLQ
items.text:
# Summary by error category and severity
curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/dlq/summary

# Individual events, with optional filtering
curl "http://localhost:8000/api/stream/dlq/events?limit=100&category=connection_timeout"

Summary response: 

{
  "size": 15,
  "by_category": {
    "connection_timeout": 10,
    "throttling": 5
  },
  "by_severity": {
    "transient": 12,
    "permanent": 3
  },
  "oldest_entry": "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z",
  "newest_entry": "2024-01-15T10:45:00Z"
}

items.title: Retry Failed Events
items.text:
# Retry DLQ events (body is the max retry attempts per event)
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/stream/dlq/retry \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '3'

Returns statistics about the retry operation (retried, succeeded, failed counts).

items.title: Low Throughput (Development)
items.text:
{
  "realtime": {
    "emit_interval_ms": 2000,
    "burst": 50,
    "max_batch_size": 128
  }
}

Throughput: ~25 events/sec

items.title: Medium Throughput (Default)
items.text:
{
  "realtime": {
    "emit_interval_ms": 500,
    "burst": 100,
    "max_batch_size": 256
  }
}

Throughput: ~200 events/sec

items.title: High Throughput (Production)
items.text:
{
  "realtime": {
    "emit_interval_ms": 100,
    "burst": 500,
    "max_batch_size": 1000
  }
}

Throughput: ~5000 events/sec

Warning: High throughput may hit target throttling limits. Monitor Azure metrics.

items.title: Legacy Target Limits
items.text:
Standard Tier: - Ingress: 1 MB/sec or 1000 events/sec - Egress: 2 MB/sec - Throughput Units: 1-20 (auto-inflate available)

Premium Tier: - Ingress: Higher throughput - Dedicated capacity - Better latency

Calculate event size: 

# Average event size
curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/statistics | jq '(.bytes_sent / .events_sent_successfully) / 1024'

items.title: Batch Optimization
items.text:
Rule of thumb: - Small events (<1KB): max_batch_size: 500-1000 - Medium events (1-10KB): max_batch_size: 100-500 - Large events (>10KB): max_batch_size: 50-100

Test batch performance: 

# Monitor batches sent
watch -n 1 'curl -s http://localhost:8000/api/stream/statistics | jq ".batches_sent, .events_sent_successfully"'

items.title: Recommended Configuration
items.text:
{
  "realtime": {
    "emit_interval_ms": 1000,
    "burst": 100,
    "max_batch_size": 256,
    "batch_timeout_ms": 1000,
    "retry_attempts": 3,
    "backoff_multiplier": 2.0,
    "circuit_breaker_enabled": true,
    "monitoring_interval": 30,
    "max_buffer_size": 10000,
    "enable_dead_letter_queue": true
  }
}

items.title: Environment Variables
items.text:
# Required
export AZURE_EVENTHUB_CONNECTION_STRING="Endpoint=sb://..."

# Optional
export ALLOWED_ORIGINS="https://your-domain.com"   # CORS origins (comma-separated)
export AZURE_STORAGE_ACCOUNT_URI="https://<account>.blob.core.windows.net"
export AZURE_STORAGE_ACCOUNT_KEY="..."

Worker count and CPU limits are configured in config.json under performance (max_cpu_percent, max_workers).

items.title: Docker Deployment
items.text:
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app
COPY . /app

RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -e .

EXPOSE 8000

CMD ["python", "-m", "uvicorn", "retail_datagen.main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

Run container: 

docker build -t retail-datagen .

docker run -d \
  --name retail-datagen \
  -p 8000:8000 \
  -e AZURE_EVENTHUB_CONNECTION_STRING="$AZURE_EVENTHUB_CONNECTION_STRING" \
  retail-datagen

items.title: Kubernetes Deployment
items.text:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: retail-datagen
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: retail-datagen
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: retail-datagen
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: retail-datagen
        image: retail-datagen:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        env:
        - name: AZURE_EVENTHUB_CONNECTION_STRING
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: azure-credentials
              key: connection-string
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
          limits:
            memory: "2Gi"
            cpu: "2000m"
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /api/stream/health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 5

items.title: Scaling Considerations
items.text:
Single Instance: - Simpler deployment - State managed in-memory - Suitable for most use cases

Multiple Instances: - Not currently supported (stateful application) - Future: Shared state via Redis/Database - Partitioning required

items.title: Operations
items.text:
1. Start Small, Scale Up
  2. Begin with burst: 50, emit_interval_ms: 2000
  3. Monitor success rate and Azure metrics

  4. Gradually increase throughput

  5. Monitor Continuously

  6. Set up alerts for key metrics
  7. Check DLQ regularly for patterns

  8. Monitor target metrics

  9. Enable Circuit Breaker

  10. Prevents cascading failures
  11. Allows automatic recovery

  12. Protects the target from overload

  13. Use Dead Letter Queue

  14. Analyze failed events for patterns
  15. Identify systemic issues

  16. Improve event generation logic

  17. Test Connection Regularly

  18. Run /stream/test endpoint periodically
  19. Monitor response times
  20. Detect configuration issues early

items.title: Security
items.text:
1. Credential Management
  2. Use environment variables (never hardcode)
  3. Rotate keys quarterly

  4. Use Azure Key Vault for production

  5. Network Security

  6. Enable TLS 1.2+ (default)
  7. Use private endpoints if available

  8. Restrict firewall rules to necessary ports

  9. Access Control

  10. Use least privilege access policies
  11. Separate read/write permissions
  12. Audit access logs regularly

items.title: Maintenance
items.text:
1. Regular Updates
  2. Keep Azure SDK updated
  3. Monitor for security patches

  4. Test updates in non-production first

  5. Log Management

  6. Rotate logs regularly
  7. Set appropriate log levels (INFO for prod)

  8. Ship logs to centralized logging system

  9. Backup State

  10. Export generation state periodically
  11. Store configuration in version control
  12. Document custom configurations

items.title: Prometheus Rules
items.text:
These use the actual metric names exposed at /metrics (e.g., streaming_events_generated_total, streaming_events_failed_total, circuit_breaker_trips_total, dlq_size, eventhub_connected, streaming_events_per_second):

groups:
  - name: retail_datagen_streaming
    rules:
      - alert: StreamingHighFailureRate
        expr: sum(rate(streaming_events_failed_total[5m])) / sum(rate(streaming_events_generated_total[5m])) > 0.05
        for: 5m
        annotations:
          summary: "High streaming failure rate"

      - alert: StreamingCircuitBreakerOpen
        expr: increase(circuit_breaker_trips_total[1h]) > 3
        for: 10m
        annotations:
          summary: "Circuit breaker tripped multiple times"

      - alert: StreamingLowThroughput
        expr: streaming_active == 1 and streaming_events_per_second < 10
        for: 10m
        annotations:
          summary: "Streaming throughput is low"
          description: "Only {{ $value }} events/sec"

items.title: Bash Monitoring Script
items.text:
#!/bin/bash

# Monitor streaming health
while true; do
  STATUS=$(curl -s http://localhost:8000/api/stream/health | jq -r '.status')
  FAILURE_RATE=$(curl -s http://localhost:8000/api/stream/statistics | jq '(.events_failed / .events_generated) * 100')

  if [ "$STATUS" != "healthy" ]; then
    echo "ALERT: Streaming health is $STATUS"
  fi

  if (( $(echo "$FAILURE_RATE > 5" | bc -l) )); then
    echo "ALERT: Failure rate is ${FAILURE_RATE}%"
  fi

  sleep 60
done

items.title: View Logs
items.text:
The application logs structured output to stdout. When started with ./launch.sh, watch the terminal, or redirect output to a file:

./launch.sh 2>&1 | tee server.log

# Search for errors
grep -i error server.log

# Search for specific event type
grep "receipt_created" server.log

items.title: Generate Diagnostic Report
items.text:
#!/bin/bash
echo "=== Streaming Diagnostic Report ===" > diagnostic.txt
echo "Generated: $(date)" >> diagnostic.txt
echo "" >> diagnostic.txt

echo "=== Status ===" >> diagnostic.txt
curl -s http://localhost:8000/api/stream/status >> diagnostic.txt
echo "" >> diagnostic.txt

echo "=== Statistics ===" >> diagnostic.txt
curl -s http://localhost:8000/api/stream/statistics >> diagnostic.txt
echo "" >> diagnostic.txt

echo "=== Health ===" >> diagnostic.txt
curl -s http://localhost:8000/api/stream/health >> diagnostic.txt
echo "" >> diagnostic.txt

echo "=== DLQ ===" >> diagnostic.txt
curl -s http://localhost:8000/api/stream/dlq/summary >> diagnostic.txt
echo "" >> diagnostic.txt

echo "=== Outbox ===" >> diagnostic.txt
curl -s http://localhost:8000/api/stream/outbox/status >> diagnostic.txt

echo "Diagnostic report saved to diagnostic.txt"

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Setup: See streaming-setup.md for initial configuration
  • API Reference: See streaming-api.md for endpoint documentation
  • Security: See auth-setup.md for credential management
  • Streaming Outbox

The outbox holds pending events for the realtime drain. It should only contain the daily slice generated by the outbox streaming path — not the entire dataset.

  • GET /api/stream/outbox/status — counts by status and oldest pending timestamp
  • POST /api/stream/outbox/drain — drains pending items with pacing
  • DELETE /api/stream/outbox/clear — fast reset (drop/recreate)

Historical generation does not populate the outbox; only /api/stream/start (outbox mode) adds new pending items when it generates the next day.

items.title: Streaming Setup Guide
items.text: Legacy guide for the deprecated local generator's Event Hub streaming mode. The current Fabric live path uses stream-events.ipynb to write directly to Eventhouse/KQL with the Fabric Spark connector for Kusto; see Phase 6: Optional Live Streaming.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
• Python 3.11+ (strict requirement)
  • Legacy Azure Event Hub target, if you are running the deprecated local generator
  • Connection string with send permissions for that legacy target
  • Historical data must be generated first (streaming requires existing base data)

items.title: 1. Install Azure Event Hub SDK
items.text:
The streaming system requires the Azure Event Hubs SDK:

pip install azure-eventhub>=5.11.0

This dependency is included in requirements.txt and automatically installed with:

pip install -e .

items.title: 2. Verify Installation
items.text:
Start the FastAPI server from the datagen-deprecated/ directory:

./launch.sh

Or run uvicorn directly:

python -m uvicorn retail_datagen.main:app --app-dir src --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --reload

Access the application at http://localhost:8000 (Swagger docs at http://localhost:8000/docs).

items.title: Option A: Environment Variable (Recommended)
items.text:
Set the connection string as an environment variable for maximum security:

export AZURE_EVENTHUB_CONNECTION_STRING="Endpoint=sb://your-namespace.servicebus.windows.net/;SharedAccessKeyName=RootManageSharedAccessKey;SharedAccessKey=your-key;EntityPath=retail-events"

Advantages: - No credentials in code or config files - Easy to rotate credentials - Works across different environments - Prevents accidental commits of secrets

For persistent configuration, add to your shell profile:

# Add to ~/.bashrc, ~/.zshrc, or equivalent
echo 'export AZURE_EVENTHUB_CONNECTION_STRING="Endpoint=sb://..."' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

items.title: Option B: Configuration File
items.text:
Edit config.json in the project root:

{
  "realtime": {
    "azure_connection_string": "Endpoint=sb://your-namespace.servicebus.windows.net/;SharedAccessKeyName=RootManageSharedAccessKey;SharedAccessKey=your-key;EntityPath=retail-events",
    "emit_interval_ms": 500,
    "burst": 100
  },
  "stream": {
    "hub": "retail-events"
  }
}

Warning: Never commit config.json with real credentials to version control. Add to .gitignore:

echo 'config.json' >> .gitignore

items.title: Option C: Azure Key Vault (Enterprise)
items.text:
The generator has no built-in Key Vault integration; store the connection string in Key Vault and inject it as the environment variable at startup:

export AZURE_EVENTHUB_CONNECTION_STRING="$(az keyvault secret show \
  --vault-name your-vault \
  --name eventhub-connection-string \
  --query value -o tsv)"
./launch.sh

Requirements: - Azure Key Vault instance - Managed identity or service principal with secret read access

items.title: Current Microsoft Fabric RTI Setup
items.text:
Do not create a Fabric Eventstream for this demo. For current Fabric RTI live ingestion, import and run utility/notebooks/stream-events.ipynb with:

Parameter Value sink eventhouse kusto_uri KQL database Query URI from the database details card kql_database retail_eventhouse

The notebook uses Structured Streaming foreachBatch to route by event_type and append to KQL tables through the Fabric Spark connector for Kusto. See Phase 6: Optional Live Streaming and Microsoft's Spark connector tutorial.

items.title: Connection String Format
items.text:
Azure Event Hub connection strings have this format:

Endpoint=sb://<namespace>.servicebus.windows.net/;
SharedAccessKeyName=<policy-name>;
SharedAccessKey=<key>;
EntityPath=<event-hub-name>

Components: - Endpoint: Service Bus namespace URL - SharedAccessKeyName: Access policy name (usually "RootManageSharedAccessKey") - SharedAccessKey: Secret key for authentication - EntityPath: Event Hub name (optional, can be set in config.json)

Example: 

Endpoint=sb://retail-analytics.servicebus.windows.net/;SharedAccessKeyName=RootManageSharedAccessKey;SharedAccessKey=abc123xyz789==;EntityPath=retail-events

items.title: Test Connection
items.text:
For the legacy generator, use the API to test the Azure Event Hub connection:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/stream/test

Expected success response: 

{
  "success": true,
  "message": "Connection test successful",
  "response_time_ms": 45.2
}

Expected failure response: 

{
  "success": false,
  "message": "Connection test failed: Invalid connection string format",
  "details": {
    "exception_type": "ValueError"
  }
}

items.title: Verify Prerequisites
items.text:
Check that fact data exists (required for streaming):

curl http://localhost:8000/api/generation/status

Expected response: 

{
  "has_fact_data": true,
  "last_generated_timestamp": "2024-01-15T10:30:00",
  "fact_start_date": "2024-01-01T00:00:00",
  "last_fact_run": "2024-01-15T10:30:00",
  "last_realtime_run": null,
  "can_start_realtime": true
}

If has_fact_data is false, generate fact data first:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/generate/fact \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{}'

items.title: Test Streaming
items.text:
Start a short streaming session (1 minute):

curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/stream/start \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"duration_minutes": 1, "emit_interval_override": 1000, "burst_override": 10}'

Monitor status:

curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/status

Check statistics:

curl http://localhost:8000/api/stream/statistics

items.title: Configuration Parameters
items.text:
Key configuration options in config.json:

{
  "realtime": {
    "emit_interval_ms": 500,           // Time between event bursts (milliseconds)
    "burst": 100,                       // Events per burst
    "max_batch_size": 256,              // Max events per Azure batch
    "batch_timeout_ms": 1000,           // Batch timeout
    "retry_attempts": 3,                // Retry failed sends
    "backoff_multiplier": 2.0,          // Exponential backoff multiplier
    "circuit_breaker_enabled": true,    // Enable circuit breaker pattern
    "monitoring_interval": 30,          // Monitoring interval (seconds)
    "max_buffer_size": 10000,           // Max events in buffer
    "enable_dead_letter_queue": true    // Enable DLQ for failed events
  }
}

Performance tuning: - Low throughput: emit_interval_ms: 2000, burst: 50 - Medium throughput: emit_interval_ms: 500, burst: 100 (default) - High throughput: emit_interval_ms: 100, burst: 500

Warning: High throughput may hit legacy Event Hub throttling limits. Monitor failure rates.

items.title: Connection String Issues
items.text:
Problem: "Invalid connection string format" Solution: Ensure connection string includes all required components (Endpoint, SharedAccessKeyName, SharedAccessKey)

Problem: "Authentication failed" Solution: Verify SharedAccessKey is correct and policy has Send permissions

items.title: Prerequisite Errors
items.text:
Problem: "Historical data must be generated before starting real-time streaming" Solution: Generate fact data before starting streaming:

# Generate dimension data
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/generate/dimensions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{}'

# Generate fact data
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/generate/fact \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{}'

items.title: Network Issues
items.text: Problem: Connection timeouts Solution: - Check firewall rules allow outbound connections to *.servicebus.windows.net - Verify DNS resolution: nslookup <namespace>.servicebus.windows.net - Test network connectivity: telnet <namespace>.servicebus.windows.net 5671

items.title: Throttling
items.text: Problem: High failure rates, circuit breaker trips Solution: - Reduce burst size - Increase emit_interval_ms - Upgrade Event Hub tier (Standard → Premium) - Check Azure Event Hub metrics for throttling

items.title: Security Best Practices
items.text:
1. Never commit credentials to version control
  2. Use environment variables for connection strings in development
  3. Use Azure Key Vault for production environments
  4. Rotate keys regularly (quarterly recommended)
  5. Use least privilege access policies (Send-only for streaming)
  6. Enable TLS 1.2+ for all connections (default)
  7. Audit access logs in Azure Portal regularly

items.title: Design
items.text: The deprecated local generator's streaming system uses an outbox pattern to ensure all fact table events are streamed in chronological order. The current Fabric live path uses stream-events.ipynb to write directly to Eventhouse/KQL.

items.title: 1. Historical Data Generation (Manual)
items.text:
# Generate historical data for analysis
# Does NOT write to streaming_outbox
await fact_generator.generate_historical_data(
    start_date, end_date,
    publish_to_outbox=False  # ← Does not populate outbox
)

Purpose: Bulk generation for historical analysis Outbox: NOT populated Use Case: Initial data load, backfilling historical data

items.title: 2. Streaming Mode (Automatic)
items.text:
# Streaming task manages outbox automatically
while streaming:
    # Try to drain outbox
    item = outbox_lease_next(conn)

    if item:
        # Send event to the legacy streaming target
        await client.send_event(event)
        outbox_ack_sent(conn, item['outbox_id'])
    else:
        # Outbox empty - generate next day
        next_day = last_generated_timestamp + 1 day
        await fact_generator.generate_historical_data(
            next_day_start, next_day_end,
            publish_to_outbox=True  # ← Populates outbox
        )

Purpose: Legacy real-time streaming Outbox: Populated ONLY when empty Use Case: Continuous event streaming

items.title: Key Points
items.text:
✅ Historical generation does NOT write to outbox - Prevents massive backlogs - Outbox only used for streaming

✅ Streaming generates one day at a time - Only when outbox is empty - Maintains chronological order - Prevents runaway generation

✅ Events sent in timestamp order - Oldest event first - Matches real-world event sequences

items.title: Preventing 96M Event Backlog
items.text:
The previous 96M event backlog was caused by: 1. Streaming task generated multiple days of data 2. Azure connection issues prevented sending 3. Events accumulated in outbox 4. Streaming kept generating more days (bug?)

Prevention: - Historical generation: publish_to_outbox=False (already correct) - Streaming generates ONLY when outbox empty (already correct) - Monitor outbox size: GET /api/stream/outbox/status

items.title: Start Streaming
items.text:
POST /api/stream/start
{
  "duration_minutes": 60  # Optional
}

items.title: Check Outbox Status
items.text: GET /api/stream/outbox/status

items.title: Clear Outbox (if needed)
items.text:
DELETE /api/stream/outbox/clear

This drops and recreates the streaming_outbox table (fast reset).

items.text: The 18 fact tables map onto 18 streaming event types: - receipt_created, receipt_line_added, payment_processed - inventory_updated, stockout_detected, reorder_triggered - customer_entered, customer_zone_changed, ble_ping_detected - truck_arrived, truck_departed - store_opened, store_closed - ad_impression, promotion_applied - online_order_created, online_order_picked, online_order_shipped

items.title: Real-Time Event Streaming System
items.text: The deprecated local retail data generator includes a real-time event streaming system. It is retained as legacy reference material; the current Fabric live path uses stream-events.ipynb to write directly to Eventhouse/KQL with the Fabric Spark connector for Kusto.

items.title: 🚀 Quick Start
items.text:
import asyncio
from datetime import timedelta
from retail_datagen.config.models import RetailConfig
from retail_datagen.streaming import EventStreamer

# Load configuration
config = RetailConfig.from_file("config.json")

# Create and initialize streamer
streamer = EventStreamer(config, azure_connection_string="your_connection_string")

# Stream events for 5 minutes
async def stream_events():
    async with streamer.streaming_session(duration=timedelta(minutes=5)):
        # Streaming runs in background
        stats = await streamer.get_statistics()
        print(f"Generated {stats['events_generated']} events")

asyncio.run(stream_events())

items.title: Core Components
items.text:
1. EventStreamer - Main orchestration engine
  2. EventFactory - Generates realistic retail events
  3. AzureEventHubClient - legacy Azure integration with resilience
  4. Event Schemas - Type-safe event definitions

items.title: Event Flow
items.text:
EventFactory → EventBuffer → BatchProcessor → Legacy streaming target
     ↓              ↓             ↓              ↓
  Statistics → DeadLetterQueue → CircuitBreaker → Monitoring

items.title: 🎯 Event Types
items.text: The system generates these retail event types:

items.title: Transaction Events
items.text:
• receipt_created - New customer purchases
  • receipt_line_added - Individual items scanned
  • payment_processed - Payment completion

items.title: Inventory Events
items.text:
• inventory_updated - Stock level changes
  • stockout_detected - Out-of-stock conditions
  • reorder_triggered - Automatic reordering

items.title: Customer Events
items.text:
• customer_entered - Foot traffic entry
  • customer_zone_changed - Movement between store zones
  • ble_ping_detected - Bluetooth beacon interactions

items.title: Operational Events
items.text:
• truck_arrived - Delivery events
  • truck_departed - Shipment completion
  • store_opened - Daily operations start
  • store_closed - Daily operations end

items.title: Marketing Events
items.text:
• ad_impression - Marketing campaign exposures
  • promotion_applied - Discount code usage

items.title: Omnichannel / Online Events
items.text:
• online_order_created - New online orders (ship-from-store, ship-from-DC, BOPIS)
  • online_order_picked - Order picked at the fulfillment node
  • online_order_shipped - Order shipped/handed off

That is 18 event types in total; see streaming/schemas.py (EventType) for the authoritative list.

items.title: 📦 Event Envelope Format
items.text:
All events use a standardized envelope:

{
  "event_type": "receipt_created",
  "payload": { /* event-specific data */ },
  "trace_id": "TR_1704067200_00001",
  "ingest_timestamp": "2024-01-01T12:00:00.000Z",
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "source": "retail-datagen",
  "correlation_id": "optional_correlation_id",
  "partition_key": "store_123",
  "session_id": "optional_session_id",
  "parent_event_id": "optional_parent_event_id"
}

correlation_id, partition_key, session_id, and parent_event_id are optional and may be null.

items.title: Custom Event Generation
items.text:
from retail_datagen.streaming import EventFactory, EventType

factory = EventFactory(stores, customers, products, dcs, seed=42)

# Generate specific event types
receipt_event = factory.generate_event(EventType.RECEIPT_CREATED, timestamp)

# Generate mixed events with custom weights

    EventType.RECEIPT_CREATED: 0.3,

items.title: Event Hooks and Monitoring
items.text:
streamer = EventStreamer(config)

# Add event hooks
def log_high_value_receipts(event):
    if event.event_type == EventType.RECEIPT_CREATED:
        if event.payload.get('total', 0) > 100:
            print(f"High-value receipt: ${event.payload['total']}")

def log_batch_stats(events):
    print(f"Sent batch of {len(events)} events")

def handle_errors(error, context):
    print(f"Error in {context}: {error}")

streamer.add_event_generated_hook(log_high_value_receipts)
streamer.add_batch_sent_hook(log_batch_stats)
streamer.add_error_hook(handle_errors)

print(f"Azure connection: {health['components']['azure_event_hub']['healthy']}")

items.title: Circuit Breaker Pattern
items.text: Automatically opens circuit after 5 consecutive failures, preventing cascade failures.

items.title: Retry Logic
items.text: Exponential backoff with configurable retry attempts for transient failures.

items.title: Dead Letter Queue
items.text: Failed events are queued for later processing or analysis.

items.title: Buffer Management
items.text: Internal buffering prevents data loss during temporary outages.

items.title: Graceful Shutdown
items.text: Proper cleanup ensures no events are lost during shutdown.

items.title: Throughput
items.text:
• Target: 1000+ events/second
  • Burst capacity: 10,000 events/burst
  • Azure batching: Up to 256 events/batch

items.title: Latency
items.text:
• Event generation: <1ms per event
  • Azure delivery: 100-500ms (network dependent)
  • End-to-end: <1 second typical

items.title: Resource Usage
items.text:
• Memory: ~50MB base + 1KB per buffered event
  • CPU: 5-10% of single core at 1000 events/sec
  • Network: ~100KB/sec at 1000 events/sec

items.title: Time-based Distribution
items.text:
• Business hours: Peak activity 9 AM - 8 PM
  • Off-hours: Minimal activity with maintenance events
  • Weekends: Reduced but consistent activity

items.title: Store-based Variation
items.text:
• Large stores: More frequent, higher-value transactions
  • Small stores: Fewer, smaller transactions
  • Geographic factors: Regional shopping patterns

items.title: Event Correlations
items.text:
• Receipt creation triggers inventory updates
  • Low inventory triggers reorder events
  • Customer entry correlates with BLE pings
  • Marketing impressions lead to promotional purchases

items.title: Seasonal Effects

  • Weather-related patterns
  • Promotional calendar alignment

items.title: Upload Data (Export + Azure Storage Upload)
items.text: Upload Parquet exports to Azure Blob Storage using the Storage account URI + key. Upload occurs immediately after export from the UI or API.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
• Python package (server-side): 
    pip install azure-storage-blob
  • Storage credentials via env or config:
  • AZURE_STORAGE_ACCOUNT_URI (may include container/prefix)
  • AZURE_STORAGE_ACCOUNT_KEY
  • or config.json → storage.account_uri, storage.account_key

items.title: Account URI Formats
items.text:
Any of the following are accepted:

  • https://account.blob.core.windows.net
  • https://account.blob.core.windows.net/container
  • https://account.blob.core.windows.net/container/prefix

When the container is omitted, the default container retail is used.

items.title: Upload Paths
items.text:
• Master upload prefix: datagen/export/master/<timestamp>
  • Facts upload prefix: datagen/export/facts/<timestamp>

The final blob name is <prefix>/<local-filename>.parquet.

items.title: UI Flow
items.text:
1. Generate data on the Local Data tab.
  2. Click “Upload Dimensions” or “Upload Facts”.
  3. The server exports Parquet files and uploads them to the configured Storage account.
  4. If Storage is not configured or the azure-storage-blob library is missing, the server will export locally and log a warning; the upload step is skipped.

items.title: API
items.text:
• Export master (uploads if configured): 
    curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/export/master \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"format":"parquet"}'
  • Export facts (uploads if configured): 
    curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/export/facts \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"format":"parquet"}'

Both endpoints return a task ID. Only the parquet format is supported (other values return 400). Optional request fields: tables (subset of logical table names) and, for facts, start_date / end_date.

  • Check progress: GET /api/export/status/{task_id}
  • List supported formats: GET /api/export/formats

The export status endpoint returns the task progress and (if uploaded) a summary under uploaded:

{
  "status": "completed",
  "output_directory": "data/export",
  "uploaded": {
    "uploaded": 42,
    "container": "retail",
    "prefix": "datagen/export/facts/20250115-133000"
  }
}

items.title: Verifying Upload
items.text:
• List blobs with Azure CLI: 

    az storage blob list \
  --account-name <account> \
  --container-name <container> \
  --prefix datagen/export/facts/ \
  --auth-mode login -o table

  • Or browse in the Azure Portal under the configured container.

items.title: Security Considerations
items.text:
• Prefer Key Vault or environment variables for secrets.
  • Use limited permissions for upload (write-only SAS or scoped RBAC if using managed identity; this sample uses account key for simplicity).
  • Avoid committing keys to source control.

items.title: Validation Guide
items.text:
This guide captures a set of sanity checks to validate the synthetic omni-channel dataset generated by the retail datagen. Run the queries against the local DuckDB database at datagen-deprecated/data/retail.duckdb (for example with the duckdb CLI, opening the file read-only while the server is running).

There is also an API-level summary per table: GET /api/data/{table_name}/summary returns row counts and date ranges.

items.title: Checks
items.text:
• Pricing invariants
  • Cost < SalePrice <= MSRP for every product in dim_products

  • Cost / SalePrice between 0.50 and 0.85 for most products

  • Tax checks

  • Store tax_rate in a reasonable range (0–10.25%)
  • Reduced-rate items present in dim_products.taxability

  • Recompute sample receipt taxes: |recorded − computed| <= $0.02 for > 98% of receipts

  • POS behavior

  • Basket size by store_format roughly: express ~2 items, hypermarket 12–16

  • Returns present (receipt_type = 'RETURN') and negative quantities in fact_receipt_lines

  • Inventory fidelity

  • fact_store_inventory_txn includes SALE, INBOUND_SHIPMENT, RETURN, ADJUSTMENT reasons

  • fact_dc_inventory_txn includes INBOUND_SHIPMENT, OUTBOUND_SHIPMENT reasons

  • Online orders

  • Fulfillment mix near 60/30/10 (ship-from-DC / ship-from-store / BOPIS) in fact_online_order_headers
  • Lines carry promo_code 10–30% of the time

  • Small cancellation rate present; cancelled orders have zero totals

  • Payments

  • fact_payments rows reference either receipt_id_ext (in-store) or order_id_ext (online), never both

  • Small share of DECLINED payments with a decline_reason

  • Marketing

  • Device cost multipliers approximately 1.0x (mobile) / 1.2x (tablet) / 1.5x (desktop) across channels in fact_marketing

  • Presence / Sensors

  • Foot traffic zones non-uniform and vary by store_format
  • BLE pings per visit vary by format; roughly 30% of pings link to customers

items.title: Automated tests
items.text:
The package's test suite covers most of these invariants:

cd datagen-deprecated
python -m pytest tests/

items.title: Presenter Demo Script — Retail Fabric RTI
items.text:
Audience: Retail ops/IT stakeholders. Goal: show end-to-end live analytics built entirely on synthetic data from the data generator streaming into Microsoft Fabric RTI.

Agenda (15–20 minutes) - 1 min: Context and architecture - 5–7 min: Live ingest (stream-events → KQL + Lakehouse) - 5–7 min: Dashboards and queries - 2–3 min: Alerts and actions

1) Set the stage (Architecture) - Open docs page Architecture and highlight: stream-events notebook → Eventhouse/KQL DB (hot), with Lakehouse Bronze reading the same event tables via shortcuts. - Emphasize: all data is synthetic; schemas from utility/src/retail_setup/generation/schemas.py.

2) Start live data - In Fabric, open stream-events.ipynb, set sink = "eventhouse" and kql_database = "retail_eventhouse" (leave kusto_uri blank to auto-resolve), then run it. - Mention envelope: event_type, trace_id, ingest_timestamp; payload varies by event.

3) Direct Eventhouse ingestion - Explain that the notebook uses Structured Streaming foreachBatch to split each micro-batch by event_type and append to typed KQL tables with the Fabric Spark connector for Kusto. - Open one event table (e.g., receipt_created) and show new rows arriving. - Point out the Lakehouse Bronze shortcuts (Tables/cusn/) that expose the same event tables to Spark.

4) Hot-path queries (KQL DB) - Run: receipt_created | take 5 then count(). - Run: mv_store_sales_minute | where ts > ago(10m) | summarize sum(total_sales) by store_id. - Show top products: run fabric/querysets/q_top_products_by_sales.kql. - Presence/dwell: run fabric/querysets/q_zone_dwell_heatmap.kql. - Logistics: run fabric/querysets/q_truck_dwell_by_site.kql.

5) Dashboards (optional initial view) - Open Real-Time Dashboard with tiles wired to materialized views. - Interact with store filter; highlight sub-second updates.

6) Alerts (Rules) - Open a rule for reorder_triggered where priority == URGENT. - Trigger scenario: in terminal, simulate low stock to fire reorder (the generator will emit reorder_triggered). - Show Teams message/email delivered in under 30s.

7) Lakehouse medallion - Show Bronze shortcuts: Tables/cusn/receipt_created (Eventhouse) and Files/fact_receipts (ADLS parquet). - Run (or show the last run of) the streaming-data-load pipeline: 03-streaming-to-silver appends new events to ag.fact_* using watermarks, then 04-streaming-to-gold rebuilds au.* aggregates (e.g., au.sales_minute_store). - Optionally open the Power BI report (fabric/powerbi/retail_model.pbip) on the semantic model for the historical/blended view.

Key talk tracks - Synthetic-only data, no PII — rapid iteration safely. - Schema-evolution tolerant: optional columns, preserve raw JSON in Lakehouse. - Latency targets: KQL < 2s, Alerts < 30s; show observed performance.

Backup queries (if needed) - Sales trend: receipt_created | summarize sum(total) by bin(ingest_timestamp, 1m). - Stockouts: stockout_detected | summarize count() by store_id. - Marketing funnel: fabric/querysets/q_campaign_conversion_funnel.kql (illustrative join).
path: Presenter Demo Script — Retail Fabric RTI

items.title: Use Cases
items.text: Based on the whitepaper and the generator's schema, the demo focuses on the following strategic use cases:

items.title: 1. Real-Time POS and Promotions
items.text:
Business Need: Monitor sales performance and promotional effectiveness in real-time to optimize revenue and margins.

Capabilities: - Sales per minute by store, category, product - Tender type distribution (cash, credit, mobile, loyalty points) - Promotion lift analysis (sales with vs without promo codes) - Real-time basket analytics (items per transaction, average ticket)

KQL Queries: Top SKUs, hourly sales trends, promo code redemption rates Event Types: receipt_created, receipt_line_added, payment_processed, promotion_applied

items.title: 2. Inventory Health and Optimization
items.text:
Business Need: Prevent stockouts, reduce excess inventory, and minimize shrinkage through real-time visibility.

Capabilities: - Low stock alerts (inventory below safety threshold) - Stockout detection and lost sales estimation - Automated reorder trigger recommendations - Shrink signal detection (unexplained inventory deltas) - DC-to-store replenishment tracking

KQL Queries: Current inventory by location, reorder backlog, velocity trends Event Types: inventory_updated, stockout_detected, reorder_triggered

items.title: 3. Customer Journey and Engagement
items.text:
Business Need: Understand in-store customer behavior to optimize layouts, staffing, and conversion rates.

Capabilities: - Store entry/exit tracking via sensors - Zone dwell time analysis and heatmaps - Customer flow patterns through store zones - Conversion rate: foot traffic → purchases - BLE beacon-based location tracking

KQL Queries: Zone occupancy, dwell distributions, conversion funnels Event Types: customer_entered, customer_zone_changed, ble_ping_detected

items.title: 4. Supply Chain and Logistics
items.text:
Business Need: Ensure on-time deliveries, optimize truck utilization, and reduce supply chain disruptions.

Capabilities: - Truck arrival/departure gate tracking - Dwell time monitoring (time at DC or store dock) - On-time delivery SLA compliance - Lane performance analysis (route efficiency) - Refrigerated transport monitoring

KQL Queries: Truck dwell KPIs, late arrival alerts, shipment status Event Types: truck_arrived, truck_departed

items.title: 5. Marketing Attribution and ROAS
items.text:
Business Need: Measure marketing campaign effectiveness and optimize channel spend based on actual in-store conversions.

Capabilities: - Digital ad impression tracking (display, social, search, email) - Attribution chain: impressions → store visits → purchases - Channel-specific conversion rates - Return on Ad Spend (ROAS) calculation - Campaign performance by geography and customer segment

KQL Queries: Conversion windows (1-48 hours), attribution lift, cost per acquisition Event Types: ad_impression, customer visit events linked to impressions

items.title: 6. Omnichannel Fulfillment
items.text:
Business Need: Track online order flow from creation through pick and ship across stores and DCs.

Capabilities: - Online order volume and average order value - Fulfillment pipeline status (created → picked → shipped) - Fulfillment mode mix (SHIP_FROM_STORE, SHIP_FROM_DC, BOPIS) - Node-level throughput (store vs DC fulfillment)

KQL Queries: fabric/querysets/q_online_orders_15m.kql, fabric/querysets/q_fulfillment_pipeline_24h.kql Event Types: online_order_created, online_order_picked, online_order_shipped

items.title: 7. CPG Supplier Collaboration Portal (Future)
items.text:
Business Need: Monetize retail data by providing CPG manufacturers with real-time insights into product performance, enabling new revenue streams and stronger partnerships.

Capabilities:

items.title: For Retailers (Revenue Generation):
items.text:
• Data Monetization: Sell access to anonymized, aggregated sales and inventory insights
  • Premium Tier: Real-time stockout alerts and promotion performance dashboards
  • Supplier Analytics as a Service: Hosted analytics portal for CPG partners
  • Retail Media Network: Targeted in-store and online advertising opportunities

items.title: For CPG Suppliers (Operational Insights):
items.text:
• Product Performance Dashboard:
  • Real-time sales velocity by SKU, store, and region
  • Inventory levels and days of supply
  • Out-of-stock alerts with lost sales estimates

  • Competitive set benchmarking (anonymized)

  • Promotion Effectiveness:

  • Lift analysis for trade promotions and displays
  • Week-over-week performance comparison
  • ROI on promotional investments

  • Geographic hotspots and underperforming markets

  • Supply Chain Collaboration:

  • Visibility into retailer DC inventory levels
  • Reorder forecasts and replenishment schedules
  • Lead time tracking and on-time delivery metrics
  • Collaborative demand planning

items.title: Example Scenarios:
items.text:
Scenario 1: Stockout Alert - CPG supplier receives real-time alert: "Product XYZ out of stock in 5 Northeast stores" - Dashboard shows lost sales estimate: $12K in past 24 hours - Supplier expedites shipment to regional DC - Retailer charges premium fee for real-time alert service

Scenario 2: Promotion Analysis - CPG runs 2-week promotional display campaign - Supplier logs into portal to view daily lift metrics - Discovers 40% sales lift in urban stores vs 15% in suburban - Reallocates display inventory to higher-performing markets mid-campaign

Scenario 3: New Product Launch Monitoring - CPG launches new SKU across 100 stores - Real-time dashboard tracks adoption: sell-through rate, repeat purchases, returns - Supplier identifies underperforming stores and provides merchandising support - Retailer monetizes this intelligence as premium service

items.title: Technical Implementation (Planned):
items.text:
Data Governance: - Row-level security in Lakehouse (supplier only sees their products) - Anonymized competitive benchmarks (no specific brand identification) - Aggregated data only (no PII, no individual transactions)

Portal Access: - Embedded Power BI reports via Fabric Semantic Model - API access for suppliers to integrate into their own systems - Scheduled daily/weekly summary emails

Querysets for Suppliers: - SupplierSalesPerformance: Sales by SKU, store, day - SupplierInventoryStatus: Current stock levels and days of supply - SupplierPromotionLift: Comparison of promo vs base periods - SupplierStockoutLog: Historical out-of-stock incidents

Pricing Models: - Basic Tier (Free): Weekly aggregated sales summary - Standard Tier ($): Daily dashboard access, monthly reports - Premium Tier ($$): Real-time alerts, API access, promotion analytics - Enterprise Tier ($$$): Demand forecasting collaboration, joint business planning

items.title: Business Value:
items.text:
For Retailers: - New high-margin revenue stream (similar to Walmart Connect, Kroger 84.51°) - Strengthened supplier relationships through transparency - Reduced stockouts via faster supplier response

For CPG Suppliers: - Faster reaction to market changes (hours vs weeks) - Improved promotion ROI through data-driven optimization - Better demand forecasting with retailer collaboration - Reduced lost sales from stockouts

Industry Precedent: - Kroger 84.51°: ~$100M+ annual revenue from CPG data services - Walmart Luminate: Supplier portal with real-time sales and inventory insights - Target Roundel: Retail media network leveraging customer data

Status: Future enhancement (Phase 5). Data contracts support this use case, but multi-tenant portal and pricing model not yet implemented.

Event types powering these use cases map directly to KQL tables and Lakehouse Silver/Gold aggregates.

items.title: Dashboards
items.text: Real-Time Dashboards for operations and customer experience, backed by the Eventhouse (hot path) with optional Lakehouse Gold overlays. For the Power BI report, see Semantic Model.

items.title: Important Note
items.text: fabric/dashboards/ contains two template files. Dashboards are imported and finalized manually in your Fabric workspace (replace the KQL database resource ID after import).

items.title: retail-ops.template.json
items.text:
Operational Real-Time Dashboard with 10 tiles sourced from materialized views and queryset queries:

Tile Source Sales/min by Store (1h) mv_store_sales_minute Top Products (15m) mv_top_products_15m Tender Mix (15m) mv_tender_mix_15m Online Orders (15m) online_order_created Fulfillment Pipeline (24h) online_order_* events Open Stockouts (24h) stockout_detected BLE Presence (30m) ble_ping_detected Zone Dwell Heatmap (30m) customer_entered Truck Dwell by Site (24h) truck_arrived/truck_departed Marketing Cost (24h) ad_impression

items.title: pricing-approval.template.json
items.text:
Human-in-the-loop Dynamic Pricing Approval dashboard (12 tiles) over the pricing approval tables created by 07-pricing-approval-tables.kql (see KQL Database). Setup instructions live in fabric/dashboards/PRICING_APPROVAL_DASHBOARD.md.

Tiles cover:

  • Pending review: Pending Recommendations Summary, Recommendations by Department, Top Pending Recommendations by Revenue Impact, Price Change Distribution, Confidence Level Breakdown, Constraint Compliance Status
  • Decisions and audit: Recent Approvals (24h), Approval Rate by Confidence Level (7d), Realized Revenue Impact (7d, approved only), Approver Activity (7d), Average Decision Time (24h), Top Rejected Recommendations by Reason

The dashboard supports the review → decide → monitor → audit workflow for ML pricing recommendations generated by 14-ml-dynamic-pricing.ipynb.

To use a template: 1. Import the template JSON into your Fabric workspace 2. Replace the KQL database resource ID with your database 3. Update connection credentials 4. Customize visuals and layout

items.title: Data Sources
items.text: Source Use Case KQL DB materialized views Real-time metrics (hot path) KQL DB event tables Detail tiles with bounded time ranges Lakehouse Gold tables Historical overlays and trends

items.title: Creating Custom Dashboards
items.text:
1. Navigate to Fabric workspace
  2. Create a new Real-Time Dashboard
  3. Add data sources:
  4. KQL database for real-time queries
  5. Lakehouse SQL endpoint for historical data
  6. Build visuals using materialized views for performance
  7. Set auto-refresh interval (e.g., 30 seconds for real-time)

items.title: Best Practices
items.text:
• Use materialized views for dashboard queries (pre-aggregated, fast)
  • Limit time ranges in KQL queries (e.g., ago(1h) for real-time)
  • Cache historical queries using Lakehouse Gold tables
  • Set appropriate refresh rates (30s for real-time, 5m for operational)

See Phase 7: Dashboards for setup instructions.

items.title: Data Agents
items.text: Fabric Data Agents — conversational agents that answer questions over the retail demo's semantic model and ontology. Stored in fabric/data-agents/ as source-control items (<name>.DataAgent/ with .platform and the Files/Config/... definition parts).

items.title: Agents
items.text:
Agent Data source Bound artifact retail-semantic-model-agent Semantic model retail_model retail-ontology-agent Ontology RetailOntology_AutoGen

Each agent's Files/Config/draft/<source>/datasource.json carries the schema the agent reasons over (tables/columns or ontology entities) plus the binding to its source artifact (artifactId + workspaceId). A published agent also has a Files/Config/published/... copy.

items.title: Re-exporting from Fabric
items.text:
The agents were exported from a live workspace with the generic item exporter, which reuses your Azure CLI login:

python -m deploy.scripts.export_items --workspace-name "Retail Demo" --item-type DataAgent --output-dir fabric/data-agents

The same exporter handles other item types (for example --item-type DataPipeline).

items.title: Deployment status
items.text:
Data agents are published by retail-setup deploy when DataAgent is included in deployment.item_types_in_scope. The deployment parameter file remaps:

  • the source workspace id to $workspace.$id
  • the semantic-model agent artifact id to $items.SemanticModel.retail_model.$id

The ontology agent points at a runtime-created ontology. Because RetailOntology_AutoGen is created by 30-create-ontology.ipynb near the end of the setup pipeline, the ontology agent may need a second task-flow/data-agent publish after the ontology exists. Rerun retail-setup deploy --env <env> --skip-terraform --yes or redeploy the task flow after the setup pipeline finishes.

items.title: Direct Eventhouse Streaming
items.text:
The Fabric Eventstream item has been removed. Live retail events are written directly from the stream-events notebook to the Eventhouse KQL database.

:::note This file keeps its original filename so existing documentation links continue to work. It now documents the direct Eventhouse write path, not a Fabric Eventstream resource. :::

items.title: Ingestion path
items.text:
stream-events notebook
  -> Spark Structured Streaming foreachBatch
  -> split micro-batch by event_type
  -> Fabric Spark connector for Kusto
  -> retail_eventhouse KQL event tables

The notebook template is utility/notebooks/templates/driver-05-stream.py and the generated/imported notebook is utility/notebooks/stream-events.ipynb.

Inside foreachBatch, the notebook filters each micro-batch by event_type and appends each subset to its matching KQL table with the Fabric Spark connector for Kusto:

format("com.microsoft.kusto.spark.synapse.datasource")

The connector options include kustoCluster, kustoDatabase, kustoTable, accessToken, and tableCreateOptions=FailIfNotExist; writes use append mode. The access token comes from notebookutils.credentials.getToken(<query_uri>).

Reference: Use a notebook with Apache Spark to query a KQL database.

items.title: Configuration
items.text:
• Source: Spark Structured Streaming rate source that generates synthetic retail events.
  • Routing: Events are routed by event_type inside foreachBatch; unrecognized types land in the unknown_event catch-all table.
  • Destination: The single Eventhouse KQL database, retail_eventhouse, with one table per event type.
  • Lakehouse access: Bronze streaming tables are OneLake shortcuts (cusn schema) that point at the Eventhouse tables (see Lakehouse).

items.title: Notebook parameters
items.text:
Parameter Meaning source_rows_per_second Spark rate-source rows per second. sink eventhouse for direct KQL writes, or delta for local/debug smoke tests. run_seconds 0 runs forever; positive values stop after N seconds. kusto_uri Leave blank to auto-resolve the Query URI from kql_database in this workspace; set it only to target a different cluster. kql_database KQL database name; default is retail_eventhouse.

There are no Eventstream, Kafka, Event Hub, bootstrap-server, or Key Vault secret parameters for the live notebook.

items.title: Event Types Written (18)
items.text:
• Transactions: receipt_created, receipt_line_added, payment_processed
  • Inventory: inventory_updated, stockout_detected, reorder_triggered
  • Customer presence: customer_entered, customer_zone_changed, ble_ping_detected
  • Supply chain / operations: truck_arrived, truck_departed, store_opened, store_closed
  • Marketing: ad_impression, promotion_applied
  • Omnichannel: online_order_created, online_order_picked, online_order_shipped

items.title: Setup Steps
items.text:
1. Run the generated KQL database script so all destination tables already exist.
  2. Open utility/notebooks/stream-events.ipynb in Fabric and attach it to the Lakehouse.
  3. Leave sink = "eventhouse" and kql_database = "retail_eventhouse"; leave kusto_uri blank to auto-resolve the Query URI, or set it to override.
  4. Start the notebook and confirm events arrive with ingest_timestamp populated.
  5. Enable OneLake availability and create cusn shortcuts if you want Lakehouse Silver/Gold processing.

See Phase 6: Streaming for detailed setup instructions.

items.title: KQL Database
items.text: Fabric Real-Time Intelligence Eventhouse (KQL database) for hot-path storage and sub-second queries over streaming data.

items.title: Overview
items.text:
• Ingest: Direct writes from stream-events.ipynb via the Fabric Spark connector for Kusto, one table per event type
  • Retention: Configurable by table category (see below)
  • Policies: Per-table ingestion batching (30s high-volume, 1m medium, 2m low-volume), materialized views for KPIs

items.title: Scripts
items.text:
Run in numbered order with .execute database script (there is no 05 script):

Script Purpose 01-create-tables.kql Event tables, retention, streaming ingestion, and batching policies 02-create-ingestion-mappings.kql JSON-to-column EventMapping mappings retained for compatibility and typed ingestion scenarios (one per event table) 03-create-functions.kql Reusable KQL helper/KPI functions 04-create-materialized-views.kql Pre-aggregated KPIs via materialized views 06-ml-anomaly-detection.kql anomaly_alerts table and time-series anomaly detection functions 07-pricing-approval-tables.kql Pricing approval workflow tables, mappings, and materialized views

items.title: Transaction Events (3)
items.text: Table Retention Description receipt_created 30 days Transaction headers with totals receipt_line_added 30 days Line items with pricing payment_processed 30 days Payment processing with metadata

items.title: Inventory Events (3)
items.text: Table Retention Description inventory_updated 14 days Quantity deltas with reason stockout_detected 14 days Zero-stock alerts reorder_triggered 14 days Reorder requests with priority

items.title: Customer Events (3)
items.text: Table Retention Description customer_entered 7 days Store entry with dwell time customer_zone_changed 7 days BLE-based zone tracking ble_ping_detected 7 days RSSI signal strength capture

items.title: Operational Events (4)
items.text: Table Retention Description truck_arrived 14 days Arrival with estimated unload truck_departed 14 days Departure with actual unload store_opened 14 days Store opening times store_closed 14 days Store closing times

items.title: Marketing Events (2)
items.text: Table Retention Description ad_impression 14 days Impressions with cost and channel promotion_applied 14 days Promo applications with discount

items.title: Omnichannel Events (3)
items.text: Table Retention Description online_order_created 30 days Order placement online_order_picked 30 days Order picking online_order_shipped 30 days Order shipping

items.title: Catch-All (1)
items.text: Table Retention Description unknown_event 7 days Unrecognized event types; raw payload stored as dynamic

items.title: ML Anomaly Detection (script 06)
items.text: Table Retention Description anomaly_alerts 30 days Detected anomalies: alert ID, metric type, entity, expected vs actual values, severity, details

items.title: Pricing Approval Workflow (script 07)
items.text:
Human-in-the-loop approval tables for ML-driven dynamic pricing (paired with the pricing approval dashboard):

Table Retention Description pricing_recommendation_created 90 days ML pricing recommendations: current/recommended price, revenue impact, confidence, reason codes, constraint status pricing_recommendation_approved 365 days Approval events with approver and approved price pricing_recommendation_rejected 365 days Rejection events with reason and notes

items.title: Retention Policy Summary
items.text: Category Retention Rationale Financial (receipts, payments, orders) 30 days Compliance requirements Operational (inventory, logistics, marketing) 14 days Operational analytics PII-adjacent (customer tracking, unknown events) 7 days Privacy considerations Pricing approval audit trail 90–365 days Decision audit history

items.title: Helpers and KPIs (script 03)
items.text: Function Description fn_attribution_window(target_campaign_id: string, windowHours: int) Joins ad impressions to receipts within a time window to attribute campaign conversions and revenue fn_truck_sla() Joins truck_arrived/truck_departed, computes dwell seconds, and filters unmatched or negative dwell records

items.title: Anomaly Detection (script 06)
items.text:
All functions use series_decompose_anomalies (threshold 1.5, auto-seasonality) and accept lookback_hours: int = 168. Anomalies are scored and classified: critical (≥3.0), high (≥2.0), medium (≥1.5), low (<1.5).

Function Detects fn_detect_transaction_velocity_anomalies Transaction count spikes/drops by store fn_detect_basket_size_anomalies Unusual average/max purchase amounts (fraud or pricing errors) fn_detect_inventory_movement_anomalies Abnormal stock changes by store/product fn_detect_payment_anomalies Payment failure rate spikes fn_detect_traffic_anomalies Foot traffic anomalies by store fn_detect_all_anomalies Union of all five detectors, sorted by severity and anomaly score

items.title: Materialized Views (7)
items.text:
KPI views (script 04) enforce a 7-day rolling window (ingest_timestamp > ago(7d)) to bound memory usage. Historical queries beyond 7 days must use base tables directly.

View Source Table Granularity Description mv_store_sales_minute receipt_created 1 minute Store sales, receipt count, average basket mv_top_products_15m receipt_line_added 15 minutes Top products by revenue/units mv_sales_product_minute receipt_line_added 1 minute Product sales by minute mv_tender_mix_15m payment_processed 15 minutes Payment method distribution mv_zone_dwell_minute customer_entered 1 minute Customer dwell by store zone mv_pending_recommendations pricing_recommendation_created latest per ID Pending pricing recommendations (status == "PENDING") mv_pricing_approval_metrics pricing_recommendation_approved 1 hour Approved-decision counts per hour mv_pricing_rejection_metrics pricing_recommendation_rejected 1 hour Rejected-decision counts per hour

items.title: Use Cases
items.text:
• Live receipts per store, top products, tender distribution
  • Inventory deltas, stockout propensity, reorder backlogs
  • Customer presence and dwell, zone heatmaps (BLE/foot traffic)
  • Truck gate-in/out SLAs and dwell KPIs
  • Campaigns → visits → purchases attribution windows
  • Time-series anomaly detection across sales, inventory, payments, and traffic
  • Dynamic pricing recommendation review and approval audit

items.title: Build Tasks
items.text:
1. Create an Eventhouse in your Fabric workspace; use its single default KQL database, retail_eventhouse
  2. Run scripts in order (01, 02, 03, 04, 06, 07)
  3. Run stream-events.ipynb with sink = "eventhouse" and kql_database = "retail_eventhouse" (leave kusto_uri blank to auto-resolve, or set it to override)
  4. Verify the Spark connector appends events to the KQL tables
  5. Test materialized views with sample queries

items.title: Lakehouse
items.text:
The Fabric Lakehouse stores generated retail data in Silver and Gold schemas for Power BI and analytics.

For new workspaces, use the rendered retail-setup notebooks 01-04. The older shortcut-based medallion notebooks remain available for legacy scenarios and live Eventhouse processing.

items.title: Schema naming convention
items.text: Schema Layer Purpose cusn Optional live Bronze Eventhouse event table shortcuts ag Silver Typed Delta dimensions and facts au Gold Pre-aggregated KPIs and ML outputs

items.title: Primary setup notebooks
items.text:
Rendered by retail-setup render into utility/out/:

Notebook Cadence Description setup-01-seed-dictionaries.ipynb Once Seeds dictionary JSON under Files/setup/dictionaries. setup-02-generate-dimensions.ipynb Once Generates dimensions and dim_date into Silver. setup-03-generate-facts.ipynb Once/on demand Generates all Silver fact tables and setup_run_log. setup-04-build-gold.ipynb After facts Builds the 9 Gold aggregate tables.

stream-events.ipynb is committed under utility/notebooks/ and is imported manually when live synthetic events are needed.

items.title: Legacy/core notebooks in fabric/lakehouse
items.text: Notebook Description 01-create-bronze-shortcuts.ipynb Guided setup/validation for legacy ADLS/Eventhouse shortcuts. 02-historical-data-load.ipynb Legacy parquet shortcut -> Silver/Gold load. 03-streaming-to-silver.ipynb Eventhouse shortcut -> Silver incremental load. 04-streaming-to-gold.ipynb Silver -> Gold aggregations for live data. 05-maintain-delta-tables.ipynb OPTIMIZE/VACUUM routines. 06-ml-* through 14-ml-* Optional ML/advanced analytics outputs. 30-create-ontology.ipynb Creates the retail ontology from Silver/Gold business entities plus Eventhouse TimeSeries bindings. 90-augment-and-dedupe-receipts.ipynb Legacy receipt cleanup utility. 99-reset-lakehouse.ipynb Destructive reset of Silver/Gold tables.

items.text:
• Setup Guide
